title: Opera Game
event: Casual game at the Paris Opera
summary: A famous attacking game where Morphy punishes slow development with rapid piece activity, open lines, and a decisive mating attack.
historicalSignificance: Often cited as the clearest classic demonstration that development and coordination matter more than grabbing material.

title: Immortal Game
summary: Anderssen sacrifices both rooks and the queen to finish with a mating net delivered by minor pieces.
historicalSignificance: The defining romantic-era brilliancy, long treated as the archetype of sacrificial attacking chess.

title: Evergreen Game
summary: Another Anderssen masterpiece where attacking momentum and piece activity overwhelm Black's king in the center.
historicalSignificance: One of the signature 19th-century brilliancies and a companion classic to the Immortal Game.

title: Peruvian Immortal
summary: A sharp miniature where Canal sacrifices both rooks and then finishes with a clean mating pattern on the queenside.
historicalSignificance: Celebrated for its twin-rook sacrifices and remembered as one of the most elegant miniatures ever played.

title: Immortal Draw
summary: A wildly tactical draw featuring sacrificial play, exposed kings, and perpetual-check resourcefulness from both sides.
historicalSignificance: One of the most famous drawn games in chess history and a rare classic where mutual attack outweighs the result.

title: Rotlewi versus Rubinstein
summary: Rubinstein's attack crescendos with a queen sacrifice and rook lift that have become standard tactical textbook material.
historicalSignificance: Rubinstein's finish is one of chess literature's canonical examples of overloading, deflection, and coordinated attack.

title: Game of the Century
summary: Fischer's celebrated queen sacrifice transforms development and piece activity into a long forcing attack that ends in a coordinated mate.
historicalSignificance: The game that made 13-year-old Bobby Fischer internationally famous and remains one of the most celebrated queen sacrifices ever played.

title: Deep Blue versus Kasparov, 1996, Game 1
summary: Deep Blue gradually builds pressure, wins material, and converts with a direct kingside finish against the reigning world champion.
historicalSignificance: The first classical-time-control game won by a computer against a reigning world champion under normal tournament conditions.

historicalSignificance: Frequently cited as one of the greatest attacking games ever played and a late-20th-century masterpiece of dynamic chess.

title: Kasparov versus the World
summary: A consultation game where Kasparov survives and eventually outplays a globally coordinated defense in a long tactical endgame.
historicalSignificance: A landmark online consultation game that showed how internet-era spectatorship and collective analysis could shape elite chess.
